explanation_md: After `std::move(a)`, `a` is left holding `nullptr` (empty). The `!a` check is true, printing "a is null". `b` now owns the integer value 99, so `*b` prints 99.

prompt_md: Why does `std::unique_ptr` have its copy constructor deleted?

options.a: To prevent accidental memory leaks when copying the pointer value
options.b: To enforce the invariant that only one unique_ptr owns a resource at a time
options.c: Because the standard library forgot to implement it
options.d: To improve runtime performance by avoiding deep copies

explanation_md: The entire design of `unique_ptr` rests on the invariant of single ownership. If copying were allowed, two `unique_ptr`s would both try to `delete` the same pointer when they destruct, causing a double-free crash. Option `a` is wrong because a copy would not leak — it would double-delete.

prompt_md: What happens to heap memory managed by a `std::unique_ptr` when the `unique_ptr` goes out of scope?

options.a: The memory is leaked until the process exits
options.b: The destructor automatically calls delete on the managed pointer
options.c: The programmer must still call delete manually
options.d: The memory is freed only if no other pointer points to it

explanation_md: The whole purpose of a smart pointer is RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization): the destructor releases the resource. `unique_ptr`'s destructor calls `delete` unconditionally. The programmer does not call `delete` manually, and there is no reference counting involved — option `d` describes `shared_ptr` behavior.
`*p` dereferences the managed integer (3). `p.reset()` calls `delete` on the internal pointer and sets it to `nullptr`, leaving `p` empty. The boolean check `!p` is true for a null `unique_ptr`.
